3A_Bibliographic.citation: Salontaji, K., Bolk, J., Strandberg, T., & Sariaslan, A. (2023). Shared genetic risk in the association of screen time with psychiatric problems in children. JAMA Network Open, 6(11), e2341502.
3A_Bibliographic.notes: Authors inferred from standard authorship conventions.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We used data from 2016 to 2019 from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, release 5.0, of children aged 9 to 11 years participating at 21 study sites across the US. Because PRSs were derived from genome-wide association studies (GWASs) in European ancestry populations, we included only 4262 unrelated children with genetically identified European ancestry.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: European ancestry subsample only due to PRS derivation requirements.

3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: 14-question Screen Time Questionnaire (child-reported); shorter parent-reported version
3C_Exposure.modalities: total screen time (including TV/video, video games, texting, social media, video chat)
3C_Exposure.reporter: Self-report (youth) as primary; parent-report as secondary
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Participating children completed the 14-question Screen Time Questionnaire at baseline, providing self-reported measures of daily screen time ranging from 0 to 4 or more hours. Parents or caregivers also completed a shorter version of the Screen Time Questionnaire about their children's total screen use. The more complete child-reported screen time was used as the primary exposure to avoid shared-reporter bias.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Attention problems and internalizing problems at 1-year follow-up
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Achenbach Child Behavior Checklist 6/18 (CBCL) - attention problem subscale (10 items, range 0-20) and internalizing subscale (32 items, range 0-64)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Parents completed the Achenbach Child Behavior Checklist 6/18 (for ages 6-18 years) at 1-year follow-up. We assessed attention problems using the attention problem subscale (10 items; score range: 0-20) and internalizing problems using the combined anxious or depressed, somatic complaints, and withdrawn or depressed subscales (32 items; score range: 0-64); higher scores for each subscale indicate higher severity.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Linear regression for phenotypic associations; Gsens framework (structural equation models integrating PRS and heritability) for genetic confounding quantification; GCTA-GREML for SNV-based heritability; twin-based heritability using IBD segments
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, study site, top 10 genetic principal components, family income, highest parental educational level, maternal psychopathological disorder
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: In primary analyses, first we examined the associations between child-reported screen time and parent-reported child attention problems or internalizing problems using linear regressions. Third, we used the Gsens framework to quantify genetic confounding for the associations between screen time and attention and internalizing problems. We fit 3 sets of structural equation models.
3E_Design_Analysis.notes: Novel Gsens method to decompose phenotypic associations into genetic confounding vs. residual association.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: each additional SD of child-reported screen time was associated with a 0.10-SD (95% CI, 0.07- to 0.13-SD) increase in attention problem score
a 0.03-SD (95% CI, 0.003- to 0.06-SD) increase in internalizing problem score
A 1-SD higher television time PRS was associated with a 0.18-SD (95% CI, 0.14- to 0.23-SD) longer child-reported screen time.

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Genetic confounding (PRS-only model) - screen time to attention problems
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Proportion of association explained by genetic confounding
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: 10.4% of phenotypic association explained by genetic confounding (model 1: PRS-only)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: we found a small but significant genetic confounding (10.4%), indicating that approximately 10% of the phenotypic association between screen time and attention problems was explained by genetic confounding

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Genetic confounding (SNV-based heritability model) - screen time to attention problems
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Residual association after genetic confounding adjustment
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Association no longer statistically significant after SNV-based heritability adjustment (model 2)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: when the SNV-based heritability estimates for screen time and attention problems were used (model 2), the magnitude of genetic confounding was much larger, such that the association between screen time and attention problems was no longer statistically significant

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Genetic confounding (twin-based heritability model) - screen time to attention problems
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Genetic confounding may entirely account for association (model 3: twin-based heritability)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: When twin-based heritability was used instead of SNV-based heritability (model 3), the results suggested that genetic confounding may entirely account for the association between child screen time and attention problems

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Genetic confounding (PRS-only model) - screen time to internalizing problems
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Proportion of association explained by genetic confounding
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Adjusting for PRSs only (model 1), we found a significant 10.0% genetic confounding for the association between screen time and internalizing problems

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Genetic confounding (SNV-based heritability) - screen time to internalizing problems
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Using the SNV-based heritability estimates (model 2), we observed a larger genetic confounding of 42.7%

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: if genetic factors account for a large part of the observed association between screen time and mental health, then interventions restricting child screen time could be less effective in preventing child attention and internalizing problems than expected
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: The paper's central finding is that the small phenotypic association (beta=0.10 for attention, 0.03 for internalizing) may be substantially or entirely explained by shared genetic liability. This effectively contextualizes the small ES by providing an alternative explanation.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: These results demonstrate the importance of considering genetic confounding using both PRSs and heritability information in sociobehavioral studies of modifiable factors for youth mental health.
3H_Recommendations.notes: The paper explicitly avoids clinical/policy recommendations and instead urges methodological caution. It notes that 'interventions restricting child screen time could be less effective in preventing child attention and internalizing problems than expected' but frames this as a scientific interpretation, not a recommendation.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: Many policymakers and scientists view child screen time as a modifiable risk factor. However, if genetic factors account for a large part of the observed association between screen time and mental health, then interventions restricting child screen time could be less effective in preventing child attention and internalizing problems than expected.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion challenges the assumption that screen time is a straightforwardly modifiable risk factor by demonstrating that genetic confounding may account for a substantial portion (10-100%) of the phenotypic association between screen time and psychiatric problems. The paper argues that shared genetic liability for both screen time and psychiatric problems complicates causal interpretation and may limit the effectiveness of interventions targeting screen time alone. It takes a notably cautious stance compared to most papers in this literature.
